title: Afternoon Delight

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Michael was using the Bluth Company crane to retrieve the family-owned frozen banana stand after it had been dumped in the bay, a holiday tradition among the local youth.'
jokes.setup: Establishing the banana stand retrieval as a serious family task
jokes.punchline: Casually noting that local youth dumping it in the bay is a 'holiday tradition,' framing vandalism as quaint seasonal custom
jokes.explanation: The narrator's deadpan framing of destruction as tradition is a hallmark AD move. Economy is excellent — the joke is entirely in the word 'tradition.' Solid opener that sets the show's ironic tone.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Another one of Dad's suits?' Michael: 'Hey, I'm the President now. Filling Dad's shoes. Literally. Except his shoes don't fit. But I did finally got into Dad's pants. Although I had to have the crotch taken out a little bit.'
jokes.setup: Gob explaining he's wearing their father's suits now that he's President of the company
jokes.punchline: The explanation escalates from 'filling his shoes' (dignified metaphor) to 'got into Dad's pants' and 'had to have the crotch taken out' — increasingly undignified physical details delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: Perfectly structured escalation from dignified metaphor to absurd physical reality. 'I had to have the crotch taken out a little bit' lands hard because Gob says it with zero awareness. Only Gob could deliver this — it's fully character-integrated. The literalism trap (shoes/pants) is a classic AD structure.

jokes.text: Gob reacts to Michael's concern about alienating employees: 'What? Oh! That's great... The President has to worry about alienating the employees now.'
jokes.setup: Michael warns Gob that he may be alienating employees
jokes.punchline: Gob treats this as a ludicrous suggestion — the President worrying about employees — revealing his complete detachment from normal workplace dynamics
jokes.explanation: Solid Gob character moment — his obliviousness about basic workplace norms is funny and consistent. The sarcasm lands because of his self-importance. Not a standout but functional character comedy.

jokes.text: Escalating suit price gag: '$3,000 suit... $4,000 suit... $5,000 suit... Come on!' shown in flashback as Gob alienates employees
jokes.setup: Gob's claim that he's not alienating employees is immediately undercut by flashbacks
jokes.punchline: Each flashback shows the suit price inflating ($3K → $4K → $5K) while his sense of grievance escalates with it — the absurdity of 'Why don't I just take a whiz through this $5,000 suit?!' as a complaint is the peak
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's standout gags. The three-beat escalation of the suit price paired with increasingly irrational grievances is perfectly structured. 'Why don't I just take a whiz through this $5,000 suit?!' is a genuinely quotable line — the logic is absurd but somehow internally consistent with Gob's character. The juxtaposition with his confident denial moments before makes it funnier. Structure score is very high because the rule-of-three with ascending numbers is textbook comic escalation executed flawlessly.

jokes.text: Narrator describes a past Christmas party: Michael turning down gifts and directing them to charity barrel while telling an employee 'We just can't have them resent us.'
jokes.setup: Michael claims he doesn't take Christmas parties too seriously
jokes.punchline: Cut to flashback of Michael being extremely uptight and self-righteous at a party — 'we just can't have them resent us' while rejecting gifts
jokes.explanation: Good structural joke — narrator announces Michael 'tends to take parties seriously' and we see exactly what that means. The visual of Michael refusing gifts while righteously directing them to the charity barrel is a solid character beat. Not as sharp as the best jokes but functional and character-specific.

jokes.text: Gob upon hearing 'George has been into the kitty': 'Into the kitty! He is awesome. Meow.'
jokes.setup: Someone uses 'into the kitty' as a financial euphemism for embezzlement
jokes.punchline: Gob takes it literally as a sexual/cat reference, responding with an admiring 'Meow'
jokes.explanation: Quick wordplay moment that exploits Gob's inability to track context. The 'Meow' is a nice economy beat. Slightly cheap (double-meaning pun is simple), but the character delivery elevates it.

jokes.text: Lucille to Lindsay about bringing her to the party: 'You see, if I show up with you, it'll just make me seem like I'm a mother.' Lindsay: 'I've never thought of you that way.' Lucille: 'That's sweet.'
jokes.setup: Lucille explains she doesn't want to bring Lindsay to the party
jokes.punchline: The reason is that Lindsay's presence would make her look like a mother. Lindsay affirms she's never thought of Lucille as a mother. Lucille takes this as a compliment.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed three-line exchange. The setup is already a dark joke (Lucille doesn't want to appear maternal), but the punchline requires Lindsay to inadvertently confirm she's never seen Lucille as a mother — which should be devastating — and then Lucille's 'That's sweet' treats the insult as a compliment. The mutual dysfunction is perfectly encapsulated. Could only be this mother-daughter pair. Earns its high craft score.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'If I show up with you, it'll just make me seem like I'm a mother.' Michael: 'I've never thought of you that way.' Lindsay: 'That's sweet.'
jokes.setup: Lucille (not Lindsay — this is actually Lucille's line; Michael replies to Lucille)
jokes.punchline: Michael's 'I've never thought of you that way' is technically a devastating insult — confirming she doesn't seem like a mother — but both characters take it as a compliment
jokes.explanation: Brilliant economy: Michael intends the line as reassurance, Lucille receives it as a compliment, but the audience hears it as the brutal truth that she has never been motherly. Three layers of meaning in seven words. 'That's sweet' from Lucille perfectly caps it — she's delighted to be told she doesn't seem like a mother. Pure AD double-meaning construction.

jokes.text: Tobias: 'I will be head to toe in blue makeup until 8:01 in case the Blue Man Group needs their understudy.'
jokes.setup: Tobias explaining why he can't attend the party
jokes.punchline: He's keeping himself available as an understudy for the Blue Man Group — a preposterous theatrical delusion — and has a specific time cutoff (8:01) suggesting this is a recurring, scheduled practice
jokes.explanation: The '8:01' detail is the key comedic addition — it implies this is a scheduled, recurring delusion with specific logistics. Pure Tobias: an absurd theatrical aspiration treated with complete earnestness. Rewatch bonus because the Blue Man Group thread pays off at episode's end.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'No, you have to go, so when men find out we're in an open relationship, they'll see you're no threat. People hear the name Tobias, they think-- big black guy.' Tobias: 'Well, obviously, I'm not a big guy. I'm not a Carl Weathers, par example.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay wants Tobias at the party so men know she's available; she says 'people hear Tobias, they think big black guy'
jokes.punchline: Tobias's rebuttal focuses only on his size ('I'm not a big guy'), not his race, and then offers 'I'm not a Carl Weathers' as a specific comparative — both missing the point hilariously
jokes.explanation: Multi-layered joke: Lindsay's casual racism, Tobias's selective parsing of the insult, and the random specificity of 'Carl Weathers' as a reference point. The obliviousness is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'I forgot about Carl. Is he still single?'
jokes.setup: Tobias mentions Carl Weathers as a size comparison
jokes.punchline: Lindsay immediately pivots to asking if Carl Weathers is romantically available, revealing she views everyone purely as a potential conquest
jokes.explanation: Five words of perfect economy. The pivot is instant and surprising — Lindsay doesn't register any other part of the conversation, just sniffs out a potential date. Entirely character-specific.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I can't. I'm supposed to go to Ann's parents' Christmas party. We're making yams.' Michael: 'Tonight? Tonight is the office party.'
jokes.setup: Michael wants George Michael to help with the banana stand and office party
jokes.punchline: George Michael has chosen yam-making with Ann's family over the office party — 'We're making yams' delivered with complete earnestness as if it's equivalent
jokes.explanation: Functional joke about George Michael's devotion to the deeply plain Ann and her family. 'We're making yams' is funny in its mundanity. Not exceptional but it seeds the Ann characterization well.

jokes.text: Michael: 'I think Ann's parents are having their party on Bethlehem time, which is pretty early.' / Michael later: 'You be with Yam. It's fine.'
jokes.setup: George Michael defends Ann's party timing by saying they're on 'Bethlehem time'
jokes.punchline: Michael first sarcastically plays along ('Bethlehem time'), then accidentally calls Ann 'Yam' instead of 'Ann' — a Freudian slip revealing his true feelings about her
jokes.explanation: Two beats working together: the 'Bethlehem time' absurdism and the 'Yam' slip. The latter is a brilliant character moment — Michael's unconscious conflation of Ann with the yams they're making reveals how little he differentiates her from the bland activity. Rewatch bonus for the layering.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'When I thought your first wife was driving us apart, I did not make a stink.' Michael: 'You complained all the time, and she was my only wife. And she died.' Lucille: 'Yeah, well. See, things have a way of working themselves out.'
jokes.setup: Lucille claims she stayed out of Michael's previous marriage as a model of restraint
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects the record (she complained constantly, the wife died), and Lucille responds with cheerful fatalism — 'things have a way of working themselves out' — implying the wife's death was a satisfying resolution
jokes.explanation: A genuinely dark and excellent joke. The structure is perfect: Lucille's false claim, Michael's devastating factual correction (she died!), and then Lucille's breezy 'things work themselves out' — implying the wife's death was convenient — delivered with absolutely no awareness of the monstrousness. Deeply character-specific to Lucille. The darkness is earned rather than cheap because it emerges from consistent characterization. Earns its 8+ score.

jokes.text: After Lucille's tense phone call about a break-in, Gob says: 'She sounds tense. That means she's not getting any from my brother Oscar.'
jokes.setup: Michael says he's leaving to check on his mother after a concerning call
jokes.punchline: Gob diagnoses his mother's emotional state as sexual frustration, delivered with casual authority
jokes.explanation: The joke is Gob's casual willingness to diagnose his mother's sex life. Gets a mild laugh on first viewing, but gains rewatch value because Michael will later literally try to solve this problem by finding Oscar — proving Gob right. The callback payoff elevates it.

jokes.text: Lucille on the phone: 'First I blow him, then I poke him. Guy has no idea what he's in for.'
jokes.setup: Lucille claims to have a surprise for the potential intruder if he returns
jokes.punchline: Her threat sounds sexually explicit but refers to a pellet gun ('blow') and a poker/fire implement ('poke') — the innocuous explanation arrives later
jokes.explanation: Classic AD double-entendre construction. The phrasing is deliberately designed to sound sexual while describing something mundane (or vice versa — the exact implements don't matter). Rewatch bonus because the reveal recontextualizes it. The 'earned vs. cheap' score dips slightly because this type of joke is a known AD formula.

jokes.text: Michael examines the 'break-in' and finds the glass is on the outside: 'I doubt anyone was trying to break in, Mom. If anything, someone was trying to break out.'
jokes.setup: Lucille reported a break-in at her home
jokes.punchline: The glass is on the outside — meaning something broke OUT, implying someone was escaping Lucille's home, not breaking into it
jokes.explanation: Clean detective-style reversal with a dark undercurrent — someone would rather escape Lucille's home than be in it. The forensic framing elevates what could be a simple insult into something more elegant.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Every year, Oscar attempts the 420-mile walk from Newport Beach to Berkeley, California. In the 12 years he's attempted this, he's never made it past UC Irvine.'
jokes.setup: Oscar goes on annual pilgrimages
jokes.punchline: 12 years of attempts and he's never made it past UC Irvine (very close to Newport Beach) — the gap between aspiration and reality is enormous and consistent
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the specificity and the scale of the failure. '12 years' suggests commitment; 'never past UC Irvine' reveals it's a failure that recurs reliably. Very economical characterization of Oscar as a lovable burnout.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'He calls it a quest to recharge his chi. I call it an excuse for him to score his pot and wag his pickle at coeds.'
jokes.setup: Oscar's noble framing of his walkabout as spiritual renewal
jokes.punchline: Lucille strips away the pretense and describes it as a drug run and exhibitionism — 'wag his pickle at coeds' is the image
jokes.explanation: 'Wag his pickle at coeds' is a memorable phrase — colorful and character-specific. The juxtaposition of chi/spiritual language vs. Lucille's blunt realism is the engine of the joke. Solid Lucille moment.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Michael, a mother doesn't have a child to give herself company. Annyong was to teach Buster a lesson. And where is that little lesson? I sent him to the Milford Academy to teach him a lesson. I can't remember about what.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks if Lucille adopted Annyong to avoid loneliness
jokes.punchline: Lucille reveals Annyong was adopted as a disciplinary instrument for Buster — and then she sent Annyong away as a lesson to himself — for reasons she can't remember. Children as pedagogical props, forgotten entirely.
jokes.explanation: The layering here is exceptional: Annyong adopted to teach Buster a lesson, Annyong then sent away to teach Annyong a lesson, and Lucille can't remember what lesson either was for. Each beat reveals a deeper horror. 'I can't remember about what' is the perfect capper — she genuinely doesn't track the children as people. Pure Lucille. High character integration score is fully justified.

jokes.text: Buster, spotting the skill crane game through the bus window: 'That could be good for Army.'
jokes.setup: Buster is supposed to be going to Army training
jokes.punchline: He reasons that a skill crane game constitutes relevant military training — and this logic will derail his entire Army career
jokes.explanation: Five words that destroy Buster's military career and plant a major plot seed. The economy is superb. His childlike logic ('could be good for Army') applies Army justification to something irrelevant — entirely consistent with Buster's developmental stunting. Rewatch bonus because we know the consequence before the plot reveals it.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals: 'In fact, Buster never went to the Army. He'd gotten hooked playing the skill crane and missed his first day.'
jokes.setup: Buster saw the skill crane and thought it could be 'good for Army'
jokes.punchline: He literally stayed and played until he missed his entire first day of Army training
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the skill crane setup. The narrator's matter-of-fact delivery of a catastrophic failure is quintessential AD. Works in tandem with the prior beat.

jokes.text: Maeby explaining why she's at the banana stand: 'My mom signed me out of school early, so then she wouldn't have to pick me up later, so then she wouldn't be late for the Christmas party.'
jokes.setup: Michael asks why Maeby is at the banana stand
jokes.punchline: The chain of reasoning reveals Lindsay pulled Maeby from school purely for scheduling convenience — her daughter's education subordinated to party attendance
jokes.explanation: Functional dark comedy about Lindsay's terrible parenting. The logic chain (sign out early → don't pick up → not late for party) is clearly laid out and believably Lindsayesque. Not exceptional but gets a laugh.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I just don't want people's kids getting their sticky little fingers all over these 26,000 dollar pants.'
jokes.setup: The escalating suit price gag — previously $3K, $4K, $5K suits
jokes.punchline: The suit price has now inflated to $26,000 for just the pants — an absurd jump that shows Gob's self-aggrandizement has no ceiling
jokes.explanation: The callback escalates the suit gag by jumping from a $5K suit to $26K pants — the non-linear inflation is funnier than a predictable progression. 'Sticky little fingers' adds texture. Rewatch bonus for the callback structure.

jokes.text: Gob's sexual harassment speech with heavy bleeping: 'Please refrain from discussing or engaging in any sort of interoffice [bleep]ing or [bleep]ing of finger [bleep] or [bleep]sting or [bleep]ing or even [bleep].'
jokes.setup: Gob announces he's given his 'big sexual harassment speech' earlier that day and recaps it
jokes.punchline: The bleeping obscures the specific acts, making them both dirtier and funnier in the imagination; 'or even [bleep]' as a final escalation implies something uniquely shocking; the list format suggests comprehensive coverage of every possible scenario
jokes.explanation: Brilliant use of the FCC bleep as a comedic tool. The joke works because the bleeping invites the audience to imagine increasingly specific acts, making their own imagination the punchline. 'Or even [bleep]' implies something beyond the prior list. The cataloguing format is inherently funny. Then 'Even though so many people in this office are begging for it' flips the conventional HR message entirely. High originality for weaponizing censorship as comedy.

jokes.text: Gob: 'And if anybody does anything with my sister Lindsay, I'll take off my pants, I'll shave my [bleep], and I'll personally [bleep]...' Michael: 'Let's get this going.'
jokes.setup: Gob's harassment speech lists prohibited activities
jokes.punchline: The speech takes a bizarre personal turn — Gob's 'punishment' for anyone touching Lindsay involves self-harm/mutilation and some unspecified personal act, which is clearly not a legitimate HR threat. Michael cuts him off.
jokes.explanation: The escalation from professional HR speech to deeply personal (and apparently self-punishing?) threat about Lindsay is delightfully unhinged. Michael's flat 'Let's get this going' as a hard cut is the perfect straight-man button. The bleeping leaves the threat magnificently unclear.

jokes.text: Gob announces the party has started, plays 'Everybody Dance Now' — and the party is clearly dead: minimal response, no one engages.
jokes.setup: Gob builds up the party start with authority
jokes.punchline: The music blares but the crowd response is visually flat — Gob's enthusiasm versus the reality of a workplace party under a terrible boss
jokes.explanation: The gap between Gob's self-image as a fun boss and the subdued reality is the running joke of the episode. 'Everybody Dance Now' is a good music choice for the irony. Functional cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And so, Michael started to become relaxed. And a short time later, his mother arrived anything but.'
jokes.setup: Michael is described as relaxing and enjoying himself
jokes.punchline: Lucille arrives immediately, demolishing the moment — the narrator's dry contrast 'anything but [relaxed]' is the perfect setup-deflation structure
jokes.explanation: Clean narrator irony. The juxtaposition is a bit formulaic for AD but executed with the show's usual economy. Serves as a transition joke more than a standout moment.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'In fact, Gob had stolen it [the liquor] earlier that day to cut down on the cost of the party.'
jokes.setup: Lucille announces her home liquor has been stolen in a 'break-in'
jokes.punchline: Gob stole it — to save money on the party he's throwing. He stole from his own mother to host a party for their own employees.
jokes.explanation: The reveal recontextualizes the earlier break-in jokes. Gob's penny-pinching logic (steal liquor from Mom to save party budget) is perfect Gob — he sees the theft as resourceful rather than wrong. The casual narrator reveal is quintessential AD.

jokes.text: Lucille to returning Buster: 'There's no shame in being a coward.' Buster: 'A coward? I'm not a coward. Would a coward have this?' [holds up awards] 'These are my awards, Mother. From Army. The seal is for marksmanship, and the gorilla is for sand racing.'
jokes.setup: Buster never went to Army but is trying to defend his honor
jokes.punchline: Buster presents obviously non-military awards (a seal, a gorilla) with invented categories ('sand racing') as proof of Army achievement — the awards are clearly from the skill crane
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes and fully earns its high score. The payoff of the skill crane subplot is the revelation that Buster's 'Army awards' are stuffed animals won from a crane game — presented with complete military formality. 'The seal is for marksmanship and the gorilla is for sand racing' is immediately quotable and perfectly absurd. The categories sound military enough to fool Lucille briefly. The specificity (two different animals, two invented categories) elevates this above a simple crane-game reveal. Buster's earnestness makes it devastating. Comparable in quality to the calibration anchors at the 8+ level.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'You have no idea what it's like to be abandoned by your son.' Michael: 'Do you see my son? He chose his girlfriend over me.' Lucille: 'How the worm has turned.' Michael: 'Look who feels the sting of an ungrateful child.' Lucille: 'Mother, you have to receive something to be ungrateful.'
jokes.setup: Lucille complains about Buster's perceived abandonment
jokes.punchline: Michael and Lucille commiserate about ungrateful children — until Michael lands the perfect counter: 'you have to receive something to be ungrateful,' implying Lucille never gave enough to be owed gratitude
jokes.explanation: 'You have to receive something to be ungrateful' is a sharp, quotable line. The rapid-fire mutual commiseration that leads to Michael's precise counter-strike is well-structured. High character integration — only Michael could deliver this specific observation to Lucille.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'You have no idea what it's like to be abandoned by your son.' Buster: 'Look who feels the sting of an ungrateful child.' Lucille: 'Mother, you have to receive something to be ungrateful.'
jokes.setup: Lucille complains about Michael choosing his girlfriend over her
jokes.punchline: Michael points out the logical impossibility of ingratitude without prior gift — implying Lucille has given nothing to be ungrateful for. Devastating in its precision.
jokes.explanation: One of the sharpest lines in the episode. 'You have to receive something to be ungrateful' is economically perfect — a logical argument that is simultaneously a devastating indictment of Lucille as a mother. It requires no preamble, no explanation; the audience immediately understands the implication. Buster's 'how the worm has turned' sets it up nicely. Justifies 8+ on every metric.

jokes.text: Michael leaves a voicemail for Tobias asking him to go to Lucille's. The voicemail is accidentally routed to Michael's home — where the Blue Man Group is performing for Tobias. 'The Blue Men!' / 'You have 101 unheard messages. First message.'
jokes.setup: Michael tries to reach Tobias via the Bluth Company phone
jokes.punchline: The call routes to Michael's home voicemail, where Tobias is distracted by an actual Blue Man Group performance in the house. '101 unheard messages' implies Lucille has been calling obsessively.
jokes.explanation: The 'Blue Man Group at the house' visual is a delightful absurdist payoff of Tobias's earlier Blue Man aspiration. '101 unheard messages' from Lucille is a great background joke — she's been calling 101 times and no one has listened. Layers working simultaneously.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Unfortunately, Tobias missed the call from the Bluth Company, and it went through to Michael's home voice mail.' Cut to: voice mail machine reading '101 unheard messages.'
jokes.setup: Michael calls Tobias hoping he'll look after Lucille
jokes.punchline: The home voicemail has 101 unheard messages — an absurd backlog suggesting Michael never checks messages, or that Lucille alone has left over 100 messages
jokes.explanation: The '101 unheard messages' is a great visual gag — the audience immediately imagines Lucille's voice filling all 101 slots, and this is confirmed by the next beat (the sampler of her messages). Sets up the voicemail montage perfectly.

jokes.text: Lucille's voicemail montage: 'Michael, it's your mother. Michael, pick up. Happy Thanksgiving! Michael, it's Mother. Michael, pick up.' — heard amid the 101 unheard messages.
jokes.setup: 101 unheard messages on Michael's home voicemail
jokes.punchline: Multiple messages from Lucille cycling through — including a random 'Happy Thanksgiving' in the middle — revealing the obsessive, non-linear nature of her calls
jokes.explanation: The 'Happy Thanksgiving' in the middle of a Christmas crisis voicemail sequence is a perfect detail — she saved a seasonal message and deployed it randomly. Economy and character integration are strong.

jokes.text: Lindsay tries to flirt with Ted at the party. Ted: 'This has been pleasant and professional. Good luck in the coming business year.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has arrived at the party trying to meet men; employees were warned against flirting
jokes.punchline: The employee gives the most sterile, HR-safe brush-off possible, treating her flirtation as a standard professional interaction
jokes.explanation: 'This has been pleasant and professional. Good luck in the coming business year.' is a perfectly calibrated line — robotic, formal, and completely impervious to Lindsay's advances. The irony that Gob's harassment speech has inadvertently made his sister untouchable is a nice structural payoff.

jokes.text: Lindsay to employee Ted: 'Oh, if you're worried about my husband, it's fine. He's no bigger than a Cub Scout. You could take him, and you could certainly take me.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is flirting with an employee; his hesitance (per Gob's harassment speech threat) reads as concern about Tobias
jokes.punchline: Lindsay actively reassures him that her husband is not a physical threat AND that she herself is available to be 'taken' — explicitly advertising an open relationship while inadvertently describing Tobias as pathetically small
jokes.explanation: The joke works on multiple layers: Lindsay deploying Tobias's open-relationship pitch while accidentally diminishing him ('no bigger than a Cub Scout'), the irony that Gob's threat specifically about Lindsay is immediately triggering exactly what he feared, and the double meaning of 'take me.' Good density.

jokes.text: Employees are sneaking stickers onto Gob's suit behind his back while he circulates proudly.
jokes.setup: Gob has been obsessed with his expensive suit and his standing with employees
jokes.punchline: Employees respond to the $26K suit not with envy but by defacing it with stickers — their revenge is petty and perfect
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff of the suit obsession — employees respond with guerrilla stickering. The rewatch bonus applies because knowing what's coming makes the earlier suit inflation jokes even funnier. The visual of Gob proudly circulating while being defaced is strong.

jokes.text: Lindsay on the Christmas party: 'It is the worst Christmas party I've ever been to in my life.' Maeby: 'Yep, like my last ten years.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay complains to Maeby about the party
jokes.punchline: Maeby casually confirms that Lindsay's Christmas parties have always been terrible — 'my last ten years' — throwing ten years of bad parenting back at Lindsay in one line
jokes.explanation: 'Like my last ten years' is a surgical economy line. Four words that indict Lindsay's entire decade as a parent. The casualness of the delivery (Maeby says it without accusation, just matter-of-factly) makes it darker and funnier.

jokes.text: George Michael's description of Ann's family party is interrupted by the narrator: 'The word George Michael was searching for was "creepy."' Cut to the Ann family Christmas party — adults singing German religious hymns in unison.
jokes.setup: George Michael struggles to describe Ann's religious family party to Maeby — 'they're kind of... religious... yeah, it was just kind of...'
jokes.punchline: Narrator supplies the word: 'creepy.' Cut to the hymn-singing scene, which confirms the assessment
jokes.explanation: The narrator completing George Michael's sentence with the blunt word he's too polite to use is a great AD device. The visual cut to the German hymn-singing confirms and exceeds the 'creepy' description. The economy of the narrator's intervention (one word) against George Michael's verbal fumbling is perfectly calibrated.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Hey, I was talking to the sound guy, and he said he's got this karaoke thing.' Michael: 'Yeah, let's do it. Put the first song on in the book.' — They have no idea what they're about to sing.
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael decide to do karaoke, casually picking the first song in the book
jokes.punchline: The song will turn out to be 'Afternoon Delight' — a song with adult sexual content disguised by an innocent melody — and neither realizes what they're agreeing to
jokes.explanation: The setup for the episode's central comedic setpiece. The casualness of 'put the first song on in the book' is a nice bit of character economy — neither Michael nor George Michael are music people, they just default to alphabetical. Rewatch bonus because knowing what comes makes the innocence more painful.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'They said I could leave for the second hour of silent prayer.'
jokes.setup: George Michael arrives at the office party, explaining he left Ann's family party
jokes.punchline: 'The second hour of silent prayer' — the implication that there are at least two hours of scheduled silent prayer at this Christmas party, and that leaving during the second is somehow appropriate
jokes.explanation: The specificity ('second hour') is what elevates this. It implies a multi-hour structured silent prayer program at a Christmas party — confirming and deepening the 'creepy' assessment. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: Michael and Maeby sing 'Afternoon Delight' as a duet — a cheerful karaoke performance of a song with explicit sexual lyrics, neither realizing the content. Intercut with Lindsay and George Michael each feeling abandoned.
jokes.setup: Michael wanted to spend time with his son; Maeby wanted to make her mother jealous. They settle for each other.
jokes.punchline: They sing 'Afternoon Delight' — a song about a sexual rendezvous — as a wholesome uncle-niece bonding moment, completely unaware of the lyrics' meaning. Cut to their respective children/parents feeling replaced.
jokes.explanation: The episode's central comedic setpiece and one of its best moments. The joke works on multiple levels simultaneously: (1) Michael and Maeby are completely innocent while singing an adult song; (2) the intercut reactions of George Michael and Lindsay feeling abandoned are the emotional and comedic heart; (3) the song choice creates dramatic irony that rewards the audience's awareness. The earned-vs-cheap score is very high because this joke is constructed over the entire episode — two characters finding comfort in each other, inadvertently demonstrating exactly the dynamic being satirized. Genuinely exceptional construction.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Hang on, Maeby.' [stops singing mid-lyric when he registers what he's been singing]
jokes.setup: Michael and Maeby are mid-duet on 'Afternoon Delight'
jokes.punchline: Michael's realization mid-song — the abrupt stop and 'Hang on, Maeby' — is the moment of cringe crystallization
jokes.explanation: Three words. The economy of 'Hang on, Maeby' as Michael's dawning horror is exceptional — we don't need him to explain, we know exactly what he's realized. The name 'Maeby' as the object of his concern makes the cringe specific and perfect. One of the most economical jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Let's toast me. Who's first? Tom, why don't you come up here and talk about me for a few minutes.'
jokes.setup: Gob announces toast time at the party
jokes.punchline: He frames the toast as being entirely about him, and assigns someone to deliver it — 'talk about me for a few minutes' is the request, not a prompt for genuine tribute
jokes.explanation: Solid Gob narcissism beat. 'Talk about me for a few minutes' as a command rather than an invitation reveals his complete unselfconsciousness. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: Tom: 'Uh, you certainly do love those suits.' Gob: 'Seven grand — you better believe I love them.'
jokes.setup: The suit price gag — previously $3K, $4K, $5K (suit), $26K (pants)
jokes.punchline: Now described as $7K — the prices are inconsistent and constantly shifting, revealing Gob makes them up on the spot. The self-affirmation ('you better believe') in response to the gentlest possible compliment is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The inconsistency in suit prices (back to $7K from $26K pants) is itself the joke for attentive viewers — Gob invents these figures. 'You better believe I love them' is a nice self-affirming non sequitur. Strong running gag installment.

jokes.text: Gob discovers the stickers on his suit mid-toast: 'What...? Who did this?' — His authority and dignity collapse simultaneously in front of everyone.
jokes.setup: Employees have been secretly stickering Gob's suit throughout the party
jokes.punchline: Gob discovers the defacement during his own toast — maximum public humiliation, maximum cringe
jokes.explanation: The timing of the reveal — during Gob's moment of intended triumph — is perfect structural payoff. The sticker gag has been building and lands here with maximum embarrassment. The structure score is high because the setpiece builds correctly over several scenes.

jokes.text: Tom's damning faint praise of Gob: 'I don't know him very well, but Gob seems like he'd be a really smart boss.' / 'Look, he... he's a great magician.'
jokes.setup: Gob is trying to get Tom to defend him after the sticker revelation
jokes.punchline: Tom's best defenses are 'seems like he'd be' (speculative) and 'he's a great magician' (irrelevant to being a boss). These are the most damning possible endorsements.
jokes.explanation: 'He's a great magician' as the best thing Tom can say about his boss is perfectly chosen. It's irrelevant and damning simultaneously. 'Seems like he'd be a really smart boss' — speculative, hedged, and clearly untrue — is equally good. The faint praise structure is executed with precision.

jokes.text: Gob fires Tom, then fires everyone: 'You're all fired. Each and every one of you. Merry Christmas.'
jokes.setup: Gob's toast/roast goes badly; he fires Tom for laughing
jokes.punchline: The firing escalates from one person to the entire company in seconds — 'Each and every one of you. Merry Christmas.' The seasonal sign-off makes the mass firing more absurd.
jokes.explanation: 'Merry Christmas' appended to a mass firing is perfect Gob — he has no sense of irony about his own actions. The escalation from Tom → everyone is appropriately abrupt. Character integration is high because only Gob would do this without hesitation.

jokes.text: Gob to Michael after firing everyone: 'I did the right thing, Michael. If I don't fire them, how do I teach a lesson to the others?' Michael: 'There are no others. You fired everyone.'
jokes.setup: Gob fires all employees and justifies it as a lesson to 'the others'
jokes.punchline: Michael points out there are no others — the logical flaw in Gob's reasoning is exposed in four words
jokes.explanation: 'There are no others. You fired everyone.' is a tight, elegant comic correction. The logic of Gob's justification self-destructs instantly under Michael's observation. Economy is near-perfect — four words end the debate entirely.

jokes.text: Gob to Michael: 'The guy who's dirty dancing with his niece is going to tell the guy in the 36-hundred dollar suit how to run the business. Come on.'
jokes.setup: Michael is criticizing Gob's management
jokes.punchline: Gob deflects by referencing Michael's 'Afternoon Delight' dance with Maeby as equivalent disqualification — AND reverts to the suit price (now $3,600, down from $7K and $26K). The suit price inconsistency is the meta-joke.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed callback that combines two running gags: the 'Afternoon Delight' performance and the suit price. Gob using 'dirty dancing with his niece' as a legitimate rhetorical weapon is funny and sharp. The suit price dropping from previous figures to $3,600 is either a continuity error played as joke or a deliberate inconsistency — either way it's funny. 'Come on!' callback to the suit escalation montage. Earns its 8.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Maybe we're better off with me being business-like and you being the good-time, useless party guy.' Gob: 'It got us this far.' Michael: 'Mm-hmm.'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests the division of roles between him and Gob
jokes.punchline: Gob's 'It got us this far' is meant to be reassuring — but 'this far' is a company on the verge of collapse, all employees fired, mid-scandal. Michael's 'mm-hmm' is perfect loaded agreement.
jokes.explanation: 'It got us this far' is devastatingly ironic given the company's status. Michael's 'Mm-hmm' as a response that acknowledges without agreeing is a perfect straight-man beat. Great economy.

jokes.text: Gob on the roast tradition: 'Oh, God, how they used to laugh with me.' Michael: 'At you.'
jokes.setup: Gob reminisces about being laughed at during the roast, misremembering it as communal laughter
jokes.punchline: Michael corrects 'with' to 'at' — a two-word reframe that reveals Gob has been misreading the social dynamic for years
jokes.explanation: One word substitution ('at' for 'with') that entirely reframes Gob's self-perception. Near-perfect economy. The single-word correction is a classic comedy device but executed with ideal timing and character specificity here. Becomes thematically resonant at the episode's end when Gob insists 'they're laughing with me.'

jokes.text: Michael to Oscar on road trip: 'My mom is very stressed out, and she needs something that I can't give her. Maybe a little "Afternoon Delight"?'
jokes.setup: Michael is trying to reconnect his mother with Oscar; 'Afternoon Delight' was just sung as a karaoke song
jokes.punchline: Michael uses 'Afternoon Delight' as a euphemism for 'companionship/fun' — but Oscar interprets it as a cannabis strain, and the audience knows it's also a sexual song they just heard. Triple meaning.
jokes.explanation: The episode's central wordplay engine. 'Afternoon Delight' simultaneously means: (1) the karaoke song about sex just performed by Michael and Maeby; (2) a cannabis strain Oscar supplies; (3) the general concept of afternoon pleasure/companionship Michael intends. The audience knows all three meanings; Michael and Oscar each know only one. This is multi-layered dramatic irony executed with genuine craft. High earned-vs-cheap score because it's constructed across the whole episode, not a cheap one-off pun.

jokes.text: Lucille (on phone): 'I was almost attacked last night, in my own home. I walk in and there's a colored man in my kitchen.' Michael: '"Colored"? What color was he exactly?' Lucille: 'Blue.'
jokes.setup: Lucille says there was 'a colored man' in her kitchen — the word 'colored' is a loaded anachronism
jokes.punchline: Michael calls her out on the language; she clarifies he was literally blue (Tobias in Blue Man Group makeup)
jokes.explanation: The single best joke in the episode and one of the best in the series. Perfect three-beat structure: (1) Lucille uses an archaic racist term, (2) Michael calls her out in the only correct way, (3) Lucille reveals she was being completely literal — the man was actually blue. The joke simultaneously (a) subverts the expected racism joke, (b) pays off Tobias's Blue Man subplot, (c) perfectly characterizes Lucille (both the casual racism and the literal-mindedness), and (d) makes Michael look foolish for assuming the worst while technically being right. The economy is extraordinary — five words ('Blue.' with the follow-up 'Hello, Lucille') do all this work. Comparable to the vasectomy anchor at the 9+ level.

jokes.text: Oscar: 'Well, sure. The question is, which way do I try to get it in her?' Michael: 'I don't need any details.' Oscar: 'Maybe I'll put it in her brownie.'
jokes.setup: Oscar is discussing how to give Lucille the 'Afternoon Deelite' cannabis
jokes.punchline: Oscar's discussion of delivery methods sounds sexually explicit but refers to edibles ('her brownie'). Michael's 'I don't need any details' is the son's pained reaction to what he thinks is TMI about his mother.
jokes.explanation: A strong extension of the Afternoon Deelite wordplay. 'Put it in her brownie' is a perfect second layer — innocent (cannabis edible) but sounds explicit in context. Michael's revolted 'I don't need any details' is the ideal reaction beat. The cringe comes from Michael thinking he's hearing about his parents' sex life.

jokes.text: Oscar: 'What happened to you?' / Maeby reveals Michael took her on a road trip. / Oscar: 'Oh, my ears. The doctor said I can't go to sleep for five hours, or I might die, or something. Oh, I got blown! So I can't sleep!'
jokes.setup: Oscar has apparently been injured somehow during his walkabout
jokes.punchline: 'I got blown!' refers to an explosion/air blast (consistent with his ears ringing), but sounds sexual. 'So I can't sleep!' as the consequence is the absurd medical instruction.
jokes.explanation: Another double-entendre in the AD formula. Less elegantly constructed than some others — 'I got blown' is a bit obvious, and the 'can't sleep or I might die, or something' vagueness is funny but underdeveloped. Below the episode's better wordplay moments.

jokes.text: George Michael about Michael and Maeby: 'I'm going to lie down for a little bit. But just, I-I... it's... I can't fall...' — trails off, unable to say he can't fall asleep without implicitly admitting he's bothered by the uncle-niece bonding.
jokes.setup: George Michael is upset that his dad now has a new 'kid' in Maeby
jokes.punchline: He starts to say he can't fall asleep but can't complete the sentence — and then apparently does fall asleep immediately, oblivious to his own incongruity
jokes.explanation: Functional awkward comedy as George Michael devolves into verbal chaos before apparently immediately falling asleep despite his stated concern. Mildly funny but not a standout.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'And Oscar was giving Lucille a little "Afternoon Deelite."' — Cut to Lucille eating brownies contentedly.
jokes.setup: Oscar said he'd put the Afternoon Deelite cannabis in her brownie
jokes.punchline: The narrator confirms with a wink, and we see Lucille unknowingly eating drugged brownies with pleasure — 'Mmm, these are really delicious.'
jokes.explanation: Clean payoff of the brownie delivery line. 'Mmm, these are really delicious' from Lucille while unknowingly stoned is a strong visual gag. The narrator's winking 'giving Lucille a little Afternoon Deelite' completes the wordplay chain. Well-earned.

jokes.text: Lucille wanders away while Oscar is getting more brownies: 'Where did she go?'
jokes.setup: Oscar is getting more pot brownies for Lucille
jokes.punchline: Lucille has already wandered off under the influence — the drug is working faster than expected
jokes.explanation: Functional beat that transitions from the brownie scene to Lucille being impaired and at large. Economical but not notable on its own.

jokes.text: Gob at the second Christmas party: 'Sure-sure, the guy in the $600 banana suit. Come on!'
jokes.setup: Gob is offended by someone in a banana suit at the second party
jokes.punchline: He's outraged by a '$600 banana suit' competing with his expensive ensemble — and reverts to the 'Come on!' catchphrase. The suit price has now collapsed from thousands to $600 for a comedy banana costume.
jokes.explanation: The suit price gag's final installment, applied to a banana costume. '$600 banana suit' is an absurd application of the price-obsession to the most undignified garment possible. 'Come on!' callback is satisfying.

jokes.text: Gob in the banana suit: 'What's funnier than a guy in an-- Oh! Hey, wait. Hey! Hey! You're pulling my leg off!'
jokes.setup: Gob has put on the banana suit to make people laugh; someone grabs what he thinks is his leg
jokes.punchline: The suit apparently has removable banana sections that look like limbs — or the costume design creates an absurd physical confusion. Gob panics at apparent dismemberment.
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that requires the visual to fully land. The panic of 'you're pulling my leg off!' is funny but better as a visual gag than transcript comedy. Functional slapstick.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Later, Gob was inside the banana stand and it was getting pretty hot in there, so he decided to lose a layer.'
jokes.setup: Gob is inside the banana stand in a banana suit
jokes.punchline: He's 'losing a layer' — removing the banana suit while inside the banana stand. A banana removing its peel inside a banana. The narrator's casual framing of this absurdity is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The layered image (banana man in banana stand losing a banana layer) is absurdist and visually rich. The narrator's 'lose a layer' is the perfect deadpan descriptor. One of the episode's more purely absurdist visual moments.

jokes.text: Gob watching Michael and Maeby together: 'Look at that. They're all over each other. With their little secrets.' Michael: 'Well, what can you do, right?' Gob: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael spot Lucille and George Michael at the party
jokes.punchline: Gob misreads the situation — but his observation that they have 'little secrets' and are 'all over each other' will be retroactively funny given what Lucille and George Michael are about to unknowingly re-enact
jokes.explanation: Mild joke in isolation but the rewatch bonus applies — this is a setup for the Lindsay/George Michael 'Afternoon Delight' reveal moments later.

jokes.text: Lindsay and George Michael inadvertently singing 'Afternoon Delight' together: 'And I think I might try nibbling a little afternoon delight...' Tobias: 'That's my wife and nephew!' / Lindsay: 'We have an open relationship!'
jokes.setup: Tobias spots Lindsay and George Michael singing the sexual song together
jokes.punchline: He's horrified — 'That's my wife and nephew!' — and her defense is 'We have an open relationship!' which entirely misses the point (it's her nephew, not a lover). The callback of the song applied to a new wrong pairing is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The episode's second-best joke. The 'Afternoon Delight' song has now claimed a second inappropriate-seeming pairing (aunt by marriage/nephew), and Lindsay's defense — 'We have an open relationship!' — is perfect because it's the wrong defense for the wrong accusation. She assumes he's jealous of an affair, not horrified by the appearance of incest. The structure is flawless: setup over the whole episode, perfect callback delivery, the wrong defense as punchline. Earns its high score.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'The effects of Lindsay and George Michael's "Afternoon Delight" was overshadowed by the effects of Oscar and Lucille's "Afternoon Deelite."'
jokes.setup: Multiple 'Afternoon Delight/Deelite' storylines converging
jokes.punchline: The narrator neatly summarizes the two simultaneous meanings while creating a comedic hierarchy — Lucille's drugged brownie reaction (stoned driving) literally overshadows the karaoke scandal
jokes.explanation: The narrator's summary line that holds all the episode's wordplay threads together. The deliberate spelling distinction ('Delight' vs. 'Deelite') doing narrative work is economical and smart. The convergence of all storylines through one phrase is satisfying AD construction.

jokes.text: Tobias, unable to hear due to his ear injury, fails to dodge Lucille's car and gets hit. 'What the hell was that?'
jokes.setup: Tobias can't hear because of his earlier ear injury ('I got blown')
jokes.punchline: The deafness, established as a throwaway gag earlier, becomes the reason Tobias can't dodge a moving car. His injury compounds his injury.
jokes.explanation: The callback structure is tight — Tobias's deafness was established as a joke, and it pays off as a physical consequence. The layering of misfortunes is classic AD. 'What the hell was that?' from the car victim is perfectly dazed.

jokes.text: Lucille, stoned from Afternoon Deelite brownies, driving erratically and hitting a banana peel from Gob's banana suit, causing the car to skid.
jokes.setup: Lucille is stoned; Gob discarded banana suit pieces; a banana peel is literally on the road
jokes.punchline: A real banana peel causes a real car to skid — the cartoon slapstick trope made literal by a confluence of completely unrelated character decisions
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional AD construction. Multiple independent plot threads (Gob's banana suit, Lucille eating cannabis brownies, Tobias's deafness) converge on a single slapstick moment that is also a literal cartoon cliché (slipping on banana peel) made real. The earned-vs-cheap score is very high because every element of this gag was independently established — nothing is imported for convenience. This is the kind of dense plot weaving that makes AD exceptional. Comparable to the calibration anchors at the 8+ level for structural craft.

jokes.text: Gob: 'I can't get out of here. I'm stuck!' — trapped inside the banana stand after the crash.
jokes.setup: Gob is inside the banana stand in a banana suit
jokes.punchline: He's physically trapped — the man who wanted to make people laugh is now unable to even exit the comedic vehicle
jokes.explanation: Functional slapstick setup for the crane rescue. Not a standout line but necessary for the physical comedy sequence.

jokes.text: Buster arrives with the Army crane and 'rescues' Gob — but the narrator warns: 'Unfortunately, Buster's newfound skills did not involve lowering gently.' Gob is hoisted and then slammed down.
jokes.setup: Buster has been using the Army crane; he spots an opportunity to be a hero and rescue Gob from the banana stand
jokes.punchline: The rescue works — except the lowering part. Buster can operate the crane but not safely, so Gob is dropped. The narrator's pre-emptive 'did not involve lowering gently' is a perfect deadpan warning.
jokes.explanation: The narrator telegraphing doom ('did not involve lowering gently') while Buster looks heroic is a great AD device — dramatic irony delivered with perfect economy. The payoff of Buster's crane 'training' from the skill crane machine is satisfying. Buster being physically dangerous while intending to help is fully character-consistent.

jokes.text: Gob, after being hoisted and slammed by Buster, hears the crowd laughing: 'They're laughing with me, Michael. They're laughing with me.'
jokes.setup: Earlier Michael corrected Gob: 'At you.' Now Gob is being laughed at after a painful physical humiliation.
jokes.punchline: Gob insists they're 'laughing with me' — the same phrase Michael corrected earlier — and he's still wrong, still oblivious, still happy about it. The callback is thematically satisfying.
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional and comedic peak. The callback to Michael's 'at you' correction is delivered here with full weight — Gob has just been publicly humiliated in the most physical way possible, and he's genuinely happy because people are laughing. Whether they're laughing with or at him has become irrelevant to him, and that arc — from defensive posturing to genuine acceptance of any laughter — is actually a tiny character completion. The structural pay-off of both the 'laughing with/at' distinction AND the episode's theme (Gob wanting to be liked) arriving simultaneously is television comedy craft at its best. Earns its high score.

jokes.text: Tobias, after being hit by a car and still apparently mobile: 'Hey, it's Tobias. Who wants to take me to the hospital?'
jokes.setup: Tobias has been hit by Lucille's car due to his deafness
jokes.punchline: He announces his hospital need casually and politely, as if asking for a ride to the grocery store. No drama, just a cheerful request for medical assistance.
jokes.explanation: 'Hey, it's Tobias. Who wants to take me to the hospital?' is a great line — the casualness and the self-identification ('it's Tobias') as if calling from a phone are perfectly absurd. Economy is excellent. One of Tobias's better moments in this episode.

jokes.text: George Michael and Michael, rebuilding the banana stand again, hear 'Afternoon Delight' playing. George Michael: 'Does not seem like it would be that dirty.' Michael: 'It is catchy. That's the thing.'
jokes.setup: Father and son reconcile at the banana stand; 'Afternoon Delight' plays nearby
jokes.punchline: Both are still oblivious to the song's meaning. George Michael reflects it 'doesn't seem dirty.' Michael's defense is its catchiness. The innocence is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The perfect button on the episode's central joke. After all the chaos caused by this song, father and son bond over their shared obliviousness about its content. 'It is catchy. That's the thing.' from Michael is a wonderful character line — he's defending the song that caused the whole mess on the merits of its melody. The echo of their earlier dynamic (bonding over the banana stand while unaware of larger forces) gives this emotional resonance. Earns its 8.

jokes.text: On the Next Arrested Development: 'The Blue Man Group finally calls for Tobias with a life-changing opportunity. Unfortunately, he can't hear it and his life stays the same.'
jokes.setup: Tobias's Blue Man Group aspiration has been established; his deafness from the car incident was established
jokes.punchline: The one moment he's been waiting for arrives — but he's deaf from the car injury and misses it. His entire arc deflated in one sentence. 'His life stays the same.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest jokes and an example of AD's cold-blooded comic efficiency. Three separate threads (Tobias's Blue Man understudy delusion, his ear injury from being 'blown,' his deafness causing him to be hit by a car) converge in a four-word epitaph: 'His life stays the same.' The 'On the Next' tag is itself a comedic device for AD — a fake teaser that delivers a complete and devastating joke. The economy is exceptional — nine words destroy an entire character's aspirations. This is comparable to the high-end calibration anchors.

tmdb_overview: Gob stages mass firings after being inadvertently roasted at the Bluth Company Christmas party. Michael is forced to rebuild the Banana Stand, and Buster plays hooky from the Army at an arcade game.
insight: Season 2's densest episode: 51 jokes in 44 minutes of nested misunderstandings.
